g.data-narration: Environment variables are named values that store configuration information, inherited by all child processes from parent processes.
g.text: Dynamic named values that configure the system & applications

g.data-narration: Child processes inherit environment variables from parent processes, enabling configuration sharing across programs.
System-defined variables are set by OS during boot/login. User-defined variables are created by users in shell configuration files.
g.text: Set by OS during boot/login (PATH, HOME, USER)

g.data-narration: Common environment variables include PATH for executables, HOME for user directory, USER for username, and SHELL for default shell.
Shell variables are local to the current shell session. Environment variables are exported and inherited by all child processes.
The export command converts shell variables into environment variables, making them available to child processes.
The env command displays all environment variables when used without arguments, showing VARIABLE_NAME=value format.
Set temporary variables before commands, or permanently in shell config files. Use unset to remove variables.
PATH is a colon-separated list of directories searched for executables. The shell searches left-to-right and runs the first match found.
g.text: PATH contains colon-separated directories searched for executables:

g.data-narration: env -i starts with empty environment, -u removes a variable, and printenv displays specific variable values.
Use env for portable shebangs, running commands with custom variables, and creating clean test environments.
Remember: env displays all variables, export makes them environment variables, unset removes them, and printenv shows specific values.
Remember the key differences: export creates environment variables, PATH uses colons, child processes inherit but cannot modify parent.
